transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Oops, there we go.
Hi.
How are you doing?
I'm great.
I'm sorry that we had a couple reschedules.
I was traveling last week in SF and we had an event in office and then it was my birthday on Saturday.
Happy birthday!
We figured it out and we're here today on a Monday afternoon.
The best time to have a meeting, right?
Right?
Absolutely.
Better than Friday afternoon.
That's very true.
I did have like a 4 p.m.
on Friday where I was like, oh man, this got put on my calendar last second.
So you're coming from Rochester, upstate?
Yep, just outside of Rochester.
How about you?
Okay, cool.
I'm in Houston, but I lived off the Grand Elf Stop for one year and then COVID had other plans for me.
Yep, yeah, that's how it goes.
Are you from New York originally?
No, I grew up in Montana and then...
Spent the better part of my 20s in the Bay Area and then moved out here once we were like ready to start a family and so kind of moved to the burbs.
And so this is the next part of my life.
But yeah, we just sort of put our finger on a map and ended up my I was working remotely before COVID and my husband has sort of a specialized job.
So we kind of moved where he could find something and it worked out.
So I've been here.
That's awesome.
Yeah, I've been to, do you know Wyndham, New York?
Wyndham?
I've heard of it, but I haven't been there.
It's like central New York, right?
Yeah, it's like maybe two or three hours north of the city.
But went there for like an offsite at the last company I worked at.
And my goodness, it's like so beautiful in the fall where you're at.
It's like one of the best places.
Yeah, it's really especially like true upstate, like where you're talking about, you can like take the

Train and just like be on the train for hours and hours and it's just gorgeous fall everything it's yeah it's very idyllic for sure I really want to go to Storm King Art Center that's like super high up on my on my list for next time I go to NYC oh nice I'm writing that down but I'm excited to chat growthx for my plan today I really just wanted to like you know get a lay of the land of what you're doing for content at Federato and which colleague referred you to us and then just basically give you the lay down low down on like the way we work um how our engagements um basically like happen and then like what what we've done for other clients how we use AI to create content stuff like that perfect awesome um well I'll tell you a little bit about like where we're at and how I ended up finding you guys um so our CMO recommended you um um
Recommended, asked me to look into you guys.
I'm sure that she probably heard of you from one of the VC friends we have or, you know, somewhere in those circles.
But you have an impressive customer list.
So that probably didn't hurt either.
Um, and as far as what we're, what we're looking for right now, uh, or very at the end of this week, I will be a content team of one.
Uh, so we are trying to scale.
We just had our, um, series D and, uh, for a hundred million dollars, which was very exciting.
Um, congrats.
It's huge.
Thank you.
Yeah.
We're very, we're very proud.
Um, but of course with that, um, comes great responsibility and, uh, we really need to figure out how to scale our content operations, um, without me being the absolute only, um, only driver.
And so we are an AI native company as well.
So we are trying to figure out, you know, what are we missing here?
What should we, because we are doing everything very old school, old fashioned, old marketing ways.
And we want to evolve and figure out what the new cool kids are doing and try to start doing that where we can.
Um, also, you know, is there, what else should we be doing?
How do we know we're, we're making an impact?
How do we know people are seeing us?
Um, you know, how do we measure that?
Uh, but yeah, like what's like, let's do something different and how do we, how do we do that?
So that's super vague, but that's kind of where we're at.
Yeah, that's a helpful starting point for sure.
Um, so it sounds like you're wanting to scale the amount of content you're creating, you know, that's going to benefit for AI search.
Like,
Embracing the long tail, thinking about all the different variations of the ways that people might go in and query, but you just don't have, like, the people to do it.
Have you tried using AI to scale your efforts?
How's that gone for you?
It's going fine.

I wrote a custom GPT for us, and I used it to create most of our content based, shaped by, you know, our, you know, a bunch of industry, like, internal thought leader inputs and things like that in our style guide.
And that's fine, but it still feels very what it is.
Like, it just doesn't feel very interesting.
It doesn't feel very thought leadershipy.
It doesn't feel very engaging.
It doesn't, it just doesn't scream to me conversion or even just, like, education.
Like, I'll take either.
You know, we want to educate our audience, but truth is when people, we have such a small, small audience in a narrow field.
And so when people come to our site, chances are they already know who we are.
So direct traffic is our biggest, biggest driver.
And so when they get to our site, they're either looking for information or they're just trying to learn more about what we do and how we can help them.
And until now, we've just been really just trying to feed that SEO monster and try to show up where our competitors do.
But like I said, we want to do better than that.
So scaling content, yes, but like better content.
And to your point, making sure we're showing up where we need to be in AI search and surfacing the information that people want and need.
That makes a lot of sense.
And then the last piece that will just help me like give.
Give you the most tailored, you know, description of what we do and also sort of like formulate some ideas because we're not a fit for everybody, right?
And like, I do think that there's got to be needs around more SEO content, right?
Like informational, educational, thought leadership, the best people to write that come internal.
So who would you say like your core audience is and then who are you up against that you're trying to drive attention from?
So it's sort of a tricky question because we just did a rebrand and we're expanding with this Series D, we're expanding our product line, but they haven't been like fully built out and released yet.
So I would say the easiest way to say we are handling the full policy life cycle of, for PNC insurers, we used to focus just on primarily on the underwriting aspect of that.
But we're expanding now beyond to include claims, billing, distribution, and probably a bunch of other stuff I don't even know about yet.
And so the people that we are, the buyers that we're looking at are insurance carriers and people that work at what are called MGAs or insurers.

Um, underwriting people still, um, will be part of that, but we're trying to broaden out so we're not just, like, siloed into the underwriter persona and, um, industry as it, by itself, like, the whole, the whole policy life cycle.
Um, does that help?
so you go after, you go after groups within the insurance space.
Are there any other types of customers that could sign up for Federato?
Nope, just all PNC insurance.
PNC insurance.
And, um...
Who would they be choosing you over or like switching to you from?
Competition, yes.
So Guidewire is our biggest competitor and they've been around forever.
Duck Creek is another one.
Duck Creek Technologies.
Vertifor, V-E-R-T-A-F-O-R-E.
Let's see, who else am I thinking?
Those are like probably the top three.
Majesco is another one.
M-A-J-E-S-C-O.
And then those are probably the biggest ones that do everything that we want to be doing.
Guidewire is the number one.
That's the, they're the top dog.
So that's the one that we're really going after.
Okay, perfect.
Yeah, super helpful.
Let me give you some more context on growth.
And feel free to interrupt me at any time as I go.
Great.
So GrowthX, think of us as like an extension of your team.
So we're an end-to-end partner with SEO and GEO strategy.
We typically are creating five articles per week for all of our clients.
We'll do all the staging and publishing.
So we take that off your plate.
And then we also do all of the LLM analytics.
So visibility and monitoring, which prompts should you track?
Which topic should you track?
How are you showing up in different LLMs against your competition?
And so people like working with us because we can really create quality at scale.
But I always do emphasize that there has to be a big need for content, right?
If we're going to be a great partner.
And there's got to be a big focus on SEO and GEO, right?
We're not typically creating thought leadership and content that's like what your product marketing team would work on.
Yeah.
And so our goal is plain and simple to help you show up in traditional and AI search like we've done for Monograph and several other customers that I'll go through today.
And the whole idea is, you know, for non-branded traffic, if you can show up in the answer, it's like the first sales call.
And if you can get in the answer, then you can exist.
And if people can start to get to your website from these answers, then they're much more likely to spend longer on the website.
They're much more likely to convert.
And so the big question is like, well, why should I choose GrowthX among all the other content marketing agencies out there?
And like, why is your process with AI better than what I could do on my own?
And part of the answer is just that our workflows are very sophisticated.
We process millions of tokens and make all these API calls to different tools.
We set them up to be agentic so that they'll run in a loop until we hit a certain threshold.
And also, we're making sure we take them through the most important factors for GEO.
So beyond, like, is it credible?
Is it comprehensive?
Is it current?
We can point our research to specific sources.
We refresh content every three months, at least, for clients.

And we're able to do this context engineering where we can take artifacts that we make around your products and services, your ICP, your company, and your brand, and we can feed that into the LLM so that everything that we create is coming, right, from your company in your voice to the audience that it's meant to be created for.
And then the story behind that is that our founder and CEO has made a living basically building organic content engines at every step in his career.
And at Deepgram, where he was CMO, he replicated that process, but now looking for ways to use it.
AI to automate, which 24x their traffic, 4x their revenue.
I can tell you much more about like Marcel's background, but any questions so far or like anything you want me to double click on?
Not yet.
No, this is awesome.
Perfect.
And I'll send you like a good resource for like Marcel talks about the content engines that he stood up in different steps in his career.
But the whole idea here is that, you we've got experts in the loop as we leverage our internal platform that has the workflows.
So I'll show you more about what that looks like.
But in terms of like the client facing team that works with all of our customers and we, you know, don't have anyone assigned to like more than four accounts at a given time.
But our delivery team, it's like about 70 people to date and our company is about 100.
So it's a big part of our team.
Our chief content officer, he was the editor-in-chief at TechCrunch for over a decade.
And...
We've got like SEO and content marketing folks that all the logos you see on the slide.
So, you know, our approach has allowed us to work with a lot of technical B2B software companies.
And when you think about content that we've created, you know, we've done all these different guides for Augment Code.
So Augment Code, they wanted to own the enterprise AI coding space against competitors like GitHub and Cursor.
And so, you know, one thing that we've done is we've really created content that compares the different tools, that gives recommendations for using AI and the software development lifecycle.
And all the content that you see here, we've created the cover images.
We do cross-linking.
We even have like code snippets in this content.
So it's much more sophisticated than, you know, you get from like the typical content partner because of the way that we can use this context with every piece that we create.
Okay.
That is awesome.
Thank you very much.
And yeah, Augment, they have seen a lot of traction with GrowthX.
We started working with them when they were just under 20,000 organic.
And now they're about 3x that number.
Within a year.
Wow.
Yeah.
And so, I mean, it's a hot topic, right?
Like AI coding.
Yeah.

But because we approach these topics audience first, and because we try to create content around all these different angles, you can see that, you know, for a lot of their searches, we've got several articles that are citations.
And they're also, you know, there's like five or six, maybe like seven or eight in this one alone.
And then they also are like referenced in the answer as well.
That's impressive.
And so we started off with editorial as our foundation, lovable.
We're working with, we've created these guides as well that are more catered towards solopreneurs, solo entrepreneurs.
But we also can think about like programmatic as another work stream.
So if there's a good programmatic use case, you know, for Lovable, they wanted us to create the best templates using their product.
And so we've actually stood up this templates library where for, you know, architecture blog, right?

Or like a graphic designer's portfolio or an architect's portfolio, you know, we optimize the text on these pages, but we're programmatically like using their products to actually create the template as well.
And so this is something that was like a much more deeper integration with Lovable, but it came after like we did more of that editorial as a foundation.
And now they, you know, use this.
It's content for folks to remix or even just jump right into the product.
So that's an interesting way we've created content as a partner.
That is super cool.
And then also for Ramp, you know, we have done a lot of their editorial content around like making ACH payments, you know, how do like different business transactions work.
But then we've also created this entire vendor directory where we use their internal spend data to create these pages that are basically like reviews of the most popular tools out there.
And so, you know, for Ramp, they want to be the authority with anything business spending.

And so now Ramp starting to show up and, you know, Airtable versus ClickUp searches and all the tables that we do, all the optimizations with the structure of the content that's directly benefiting our clients for GEO.
And so how do we, you know, go about this process, right?
Like how do we create content?

What we do is we start with an eight-week strategy sprint where we're developing those artifacts, we're developing a roadmap of assignments, and then in phase two, we're executing on that strategy at scale.

And so our clients, they typically get five to 10 articles during the eight-week strategy sprint, but they also see the quality that we can hit and then the roadmap that we would execute on in phase two so that you have that as a benchmark to make your decision about a longer term, right?
12 months is a pretty big commitment.
So we want to make sure that you feel comfortable with like what we would create and the quality that we can hit before we move into that part of the partnership.
So it's 30 to 40k up front for the eight weeks.
And then after that, you can decide if you want the longer-term engagement, which would range, I'm assuming, based on what kind of content, how deep you want to go with that.
Is that accurate?
That's exactly right.
If you wanted to add a custom work stream, they're typically 9 to 15K, but the 15 to 18 that you see, it represents editorial as a starting point.
And it would scale to 18 if you wanted more than five articles per week, basically.
Okay.
And when you say, so some of the content that I see, so I see us wanting a mix of blog content and then, which I assume is when you say article, blog, same thing.
But then also the guides, like longer form content, we definitely want more of that as well.
How does that fit in with this?
Is that like a separate SOW or does that...
Like, how does that work?
Yeah, tell me a little bit more about the long form, like either how many words are like, are we talking about, you know, annual reports?
Are we talking about more of like a guide that could be a blog post, but is just longer in nature?
Yeah, so I'd say it could be a mix.
So right now we have a, we just kicked off an annual state of the industry type report.
We have that contracted out to someone else right now, but that's something that we could consider having someone else do.

But what I'm really interested in is the type of stuff that's more top of funnel, middle funnel, kind of what you had showed me before with some of the guides where it could just be, it's more like a really long blog post or three long blog posts packaged up together into a more comprehensive guide that talks through, you know, all the steps to do XYZ.
That kind of stuff.

We also have some competitive takedown campaigns that are coming up that we want to maybe not explicitly call out our competitors, but speak to all of the, you know, all those comparisons and those kinds of things.
So there could be some guides coming out about those too.
And then specific product areas.
Once we expand into those product areas, having a, like, you know, the complete guide to billing and PNC insurance.
And again, it could be, I don't have an exact word count for you now, but if you, I would imagine sort of like three longer blog posts combined into one.
They don't have to be that long, but something that we would potentially be gating.
So we want it to feel, feel like it's meaty enough that people will feel like they're getting their money's worth.
For sure.
That's helpful.
It all sounds in scope, except for maybe like the state of the industry report, which is probably something that you would publish annually or semi-annually.
But, you know, our goal is basically just to be your partner for creating like the best words for an assignment and the strategy around what assignments we should actually tackle.

So if it came to something that would be more like survey intensive, right, or coming up with like original data points, right, that don't exist, that would be less our scope and more your in-house experts, right?
Perfect.
Yeah.
And it's something that we've done before.
It just comes later in the partnership.
Like we need that foundation.
We need the right artifacts.
We need to like really understand your business and your ICP before we start thinking outside of SEO and GEO content.
That makes perfect sense.
But yeah, to go into the process a bit more, so Engine is a good example because it's pretty simple, right?
Business travel.
And we've created all these business travel guides for Engine.
But the first thing that we did was we actually just dug deep into the keyword opportunity.
So it's seeing, okay, what are all the keywords that could be relevant?
What's the competitor content?
What's the search volume and keyword difficulty?
And then from there, what we're doing is we're looking at refresh opportunities on your website.
And then we're coming up with these topic clusters where we can generate content at scale and working with you to figure out which topics you want to prioritize.
And so once we've got a good grip on the topics and personas that we're writing for, then we start

We're creating this huge backlog of assignments so that all you have to do and you get, you know, a good amount of notice or heads up on what we're considering is just review drafts and give us feedback in a timely manner so that we can go in and handle the last mile execution.
And so when you think about, you know, us creating an assignment and even executing on an assignment, for every piece of content that we create, we're using these artifacts as context.
And so if you take the engine example, we found out that for them, they wanted to create content that was focused on specific industry verticals.
And so we created a personas artifact for all their target industries.
And then in that artifact, we go super deep into the construction operations manager, the finance manager, and the project manager.
And we're basically mapping everything that they might be thinking about.
With search that's relative to your products.
And then also, like, why do they choose engine at the end of the day, right?
What's engine secret sauce that can be connected to what this person is searching, what they're trying to get better insights toward?
So it's this nice blend of art and science where scientifically it's all rooted in, is there search volume?
Is there an opportunity to outrank your competitors?
But then on the art side, it's all focused on everything that could be going through your ICP's mind.
Does that make sense?
Yes, that makes perfect sense.
And something that's very top of mind for us right now, too, because as we expand into some of these newer product areas, there are new buyer personas that we're going to be going after, too.
So this will be a new area for us that we don't have any content for at all.
So it's a huge gap.

Do you, as far as like, when you say artifacts, I assume that means things that have been already published, and then insights that we give you documents, we give you battle cards, data sheets, persona, like, can you talk a little bit more about how you get that, get there from what you get from your customers?
Yeah, so we ingest resources and their brand, their voice and tone guides, product marketing, one pagers, battle card info, ICP info.
And then we do a kickoff call and a product deep dive to capture nuance.
And then we use our workflows and our experts to generate these artifacts in a combination approach, right?
Like, we can generate them with AI workflows using all these resources.
resources, right?
Like, we
Using these calls, but we also have the human review for everything that we're doing.
And so that's helping us generate artifacts.
But if you dig into like a piece of content that we're creating, I can give you a quick example here.
So take this article that we generated for Superhuman where we're basically writing a comparison to Fixer, one of their top competitors for the mail side.
We've got the direction from the managing editor, which a lot of this is human generated.
They're thinking about like, what are the logical H2s and H3s that we said we should execute on?
What are some high level instructions?
But then we're also using those personas artifacts and the company artifact to go in and for this assignment, right?
The output is a brief, but, and you saw a little bit of the inputs, but then what's happening in the background here.
And we can customize this from client to client is we're going in and we're looking at keyword traffic, organic results, SERP ranking factors, competitor content, search intent.
And then all of that informs the output for the brief.
But then from there, you know, we take this into agentic research and then article drafting, fact checking.
But what our team has the ability to do is they have the ability to change anything that's being written or give commands with those artifacts present.
So I could, you know, take an entire section and then, you know, add three bullet points catered to that are specific to, you know, the busy executive.
So it's this combination that allows us to create content at scale.
Okay.
And I apologize, I kind of lost track of time here.
Oh, yeah, me too.
I do have a back-to-back, and I'm happy to continue the conversation by going deeper into the demo.

Yeah, that would be awesome.
And we are in the midst of budget planning.
So this is good timing.
So, yeah, send me some examples, and then I will dig in on our side a little bit, and we'll reconnect if we want to go deeper.
But this looks very interesting to me, and looks like it's really what we're looking for.
just depends on whether or not I can get budget for it.

Yes, definitely.
I can bring Marcel.
If it makes sense and he's got the time available, so happy to do that.

was really nice meeting you and we will catch up.
